source_quote: Results represent a relative risk reduction of 80% for residents treated with the dressings and for every 12 patients that we treated we prevented one pressure injury.
reasoning: The excerpt mentions a relative risk reduction of 80% and NNT of 12, but does not provide the raw event counts (events/n) for intervention and control groups, nor does it state the absolute RR with confidence interval. Insufficient data in the provided excerpt.

source_quote: Of the 135 participants, 70 (51.9%) developed PI, of whom 29 (21.5%) had PI in both heels and 41 (30.4%) in only one. Among the 270 skin sites analysed, 99 developed PI, resulting in a global incidence of 36.7%. The incidence of PI was different (P = .001) between the IG (26.7%) and the CG (46.7%), with a relative risk of 0.57. Table 2: Intervention (n = 135): 36 (26.7%), Control (n = 135): 63 (46.7%).
reasoning: Binary outcome data clearly reported. Intervention group (multi-layered silicone foam) had 36 pressure injuries out of 135 heels. Control group (transparent polyurethane film) had 63 PIs out of 135 heels. RR = 0.57 explicitly stated. CI not provided in the excerpt.

source_quote: In the olive oil group, 11 residents developed PUs (all category I), with an incidence of 4.18% (95% CI 2.35-7.33%). In the HOFA group, 18 residents developed PUs (all category I), an incidence of 6.57% (95% CI 4.20-10.14%). Table 2 intention-to-treat analysis: Olive oil PU/total 11/283, HOFA PU/total 18/288, RR 0.62 (95% CI 0.29 to 1.29).
reasoning: Binary outcome data reported. Using intention-to-treat analysis as best practice: olive oil group had 11 PUs out of 283 residents, HOFA group had 18 PUs out of 288 residents. RR and 95% CI explicitly stated in Table 2.

source_quote: Sacrum: Olive Oil 8 (2.55%) vs HOFA 8 (3.08%), ARR 0.53 (-2.2 to 3.26). Right heel: Olive Oil 4 (1.27%) vs HOFA 5 (1.92%), ARR 0.65 (-1.43 to 2.73). Left heel: Olive Oil 3 (0.96%) vs HOFA 3 (1.15%), ARR 0.2 (-1.49 to 1.88).
reasoning: Data reported as percentages by anatomical site with ARR (absolute risk difference in percentage points). Denominators not explicitly stated in excerpt. Multiple sites make it unclear which outcome to extract as primary. The outcome was 'Pressure ulcer' without site specification.

source_quote: Six PUs (incidence = 6·1%) were detected in the intervention group versus seven (incidence = 7·4%) in the placebo group. Differences were not statistically significant (z = 0·08; P = 0·94), relative risk = 0·82 (95% confidence interval = 0·29–2·36). The intervention group included 99 patients, and the placebo group comprised 95 patients.
reasoning: Binary outcome clearly stated in abstract: 6 pressure ulcers out of 99 patients in intervention group (IPARZINE-4A-SKR topical product), 7 PUs out of 95 patients in placebo group. RR and 95% CI explicitly reported.

source_quote: In the intervention group 1 patient afflicted with sore of hip and two people with sacral pressure ulcer. In the control group 3 patients affiliated with sore of hip, 8 people with sacral pressure ulcer, and 1 person had pressure sore of heel. Analysis of the data showed that both groups had statistically significant differences in the incidence of pressure ulcers (P = 0.047). The mean age of patients in the control group was (42.34 ± 12.19) and in the intervention group was (41.71 ± 11.50) years, respectively.
reasoning: Total patients with pressure ulcers calculated from site-specific data. Intervention (Aloe Vera gel): 1 hip + 2 sacral = 3 patients. Control (placebo gel): 3 hip + 8 sacral + 1 heel = 12 patients. Total sample was 80 patients, so 40 per group. P-value reported but no RR/OR calculated in excerpt.

source_quote: The study protocol presents the starting points, design, and methods of the PELSTAB Study. We expect that, after 12 weeks of high-intensity PFMT, women with stress urinary incontinence will have significantly less incontinence episode frequency, better reduction of HA during contraction and Valsalva.
reasoning: This is a study protocol describing planned methods and expected outcomes, not actual results. No outcome data available for extraction.

source_quote: Post-intervention data revealed a range of benefits for women who attended MP classes and who had lower symptom severity at baseline: improved self-esteem (p = 0.032), decreased social embarrassment (p = 0.026) and lower impact on normal daily activities (p = 0.025). In contrast, women with higher symptom severity showed improvement in their personal relationships (p = 0.017).
reasoning: Results report p-values for various I-QoL subscales (self-esteem, social embarrassment, daily activities) but do not provide mean, SD, or sample sizes for the overall incontinence quality of life score in the excerpt. The outcome specified was 'Incontinence quality of life' (continuous), which requires mean and SD.

source_quote: In the intragroup analysis, a decrease in the ICIQ-UI-SF score was observed in both groups (14.0 to 10.0; 13.5 to 0), associated with increased endurance (2.5 to 3.5; 2.5 to 4.0) in G_Control and G_Game, respectively.
reasoning: Median values reported (baseline to follow-up) for ICIQ-UI-SF score: G_Control 14.0 to 10.0, G_Game 13.5 to 0. These appear to be medians, not means. No mean, SD, or post-intervention sample sizes provided. Cannot extract complete continuous data without mean and SD.

source_quote: In the subjective evaluation, only 23.8% of the control group patients were satisfied with their treatment. In the supervised group, 66.8% of patients did not want any other treatment.
reasoning: The outcome 'Satisfaction' is reported as percentages (23.8% satisfied in control, 66.8% did not want other treatment in intervention), but these are not the same binary outcome and denominators are not clearly stated. The phrasing 'did not want any other treatment' is not equivalent to 'satisfied'. Insufficient clarity to extract as binary outcome.

source_quote: Compared to enhanced treatment as usual, LGCC was not associated with reduced cardiometabolic risk factors based on 6 and 12-month repeated measures analyses. Among patients with BMI >=30 or systolic blood pressure >=140, LGCC was associated with decreased impaired functioning (respectively beta=−2.2, beta=−3.8; p=.04 for both) and depressive symptom scores (respectively beta=−2.0, beta=−3.5; both p=.04).
reasoning: The outcome specified was 'Quality of life: average change in mental health component - 12 months' (continuous). The excerpt reports beta coefficients for subgroup analyses of functioning and depressive symptoms, but does not provide mean, SD, or sample sizes for the mental health component of quality of life at 12 months for the full sample.

source_quote: Results showed that Hct levels at 15 min after CPB beginning (T2) and at CPB end (T3), and number of intraoperative blood transfusions were significantly lower in the experimental group (P<0.05). There were no significant differences in CPB time, aortic blocking time, T2-Lac value or T3-Lac between the two groups (P>0.05). Postoperatively, there were no significant differences in Hct (2 h after surgery), mechanical ventilation time, intensive care unit time, or postoperative blood transfusion between two groups (P>0.05).
reasoning: The outcome specified was 'Haematocrit (%) levels postoperatively' (continuous). The excerpt states no significant differences in Hct at 2h post-surgery between groups (P>0.05) but does not provide the actual mean, SD, or sample sizes for postoperative Hct levels.

source_quote: Patients choosing HHD as initial RRT spend less time in hospital compared with patients on IHD and PD and they were more likely than PD patients, to remain on their initial modality. These advantages, in combination with better survival and higher likelihood of renal transplantation, are important incentives for promoting the use of HHD.
reasoning: The outcome specified was 'All-cause death' (binary). The abstract mentions 'better survival' and the paper is about morbidity (hospital admissions), but the excerpt does not provide mortality counts (deaths/total) for HHD vs IHD or PD groups.

source_quote: 162 subjects were studied, 81 per group. There was a numerical, but not statistically significant decrease in total blood product transfusions (203 v. 260) and mortality (2 v. 6 deaths) in the washed group compared to the unwashed group.
reasoning: Binary mortality outcome clearly stated: 2 deaths in washed blood group (n=81) vs 6 deaths in unwashed blood group (n=81). Not statistically significant. No RR or OR with CI calculated in the excerpt.
